coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots and interfacial chemical bond synergistically modulated S-scheme Mn0.5Cd0.5S/BiOBr photocatalyst for efficient water purification
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.prism:publisher: Published by Elsevier Ltd on behalf of The editorial office of Journal of Materials Science & Technology.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Shijie
You, Changjun
Xue, Qingquan
Zhao, Yiqian
Yang, Fang
Liu, Yanping
Bai, Lina
Zhang, Mingyi
Zhuang, Chunqiang
coredata.dc:description: Devising robust S-scheme photocatalysts is of central importance for achieving high-efficient micropollutant decontamination. However, the conscious optimization of S-scheme system with high performance remains a prime challenge. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CDs) and Mn0.5Cd0.5S (MCS) are mounted on BiOBr (BOB) microspheres, establishing an advanced S-scheme heterojunction with interfacial Bi-S bond. The interfacial Bi-S bonds function as superb channels at atomic-scale to abate the energy barrier for S-scheme charge transportation. Meanwhile, CDs serve as electron collectors to preserve highly reductive electrons from MCS, further augmenting the spatial separation of photo-carriers. Therefore, the optimized CDs/MCS/BOB (MBC) heterojunction manifests significantly strengthened tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) destruction activity and its reaction rate constant is approximately 3.1, 2.2, 2.1, and 1.5 folds that than that of MCS, BOB, BOB/CDs and MCS/BOB. In addition, MBC exhibits high stability and significant resistance to environmental interferences. The toxicology evaluation confirms the effective abatement of toxicity of TC after treatment. This achievement demonstrates the benefits of CDs-optimized S-scheme photosystems with chemical bonds for photocatalytic water decontamination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Interfacial chemical bond
Carbon quantum dots
S-scheme heterojunction
Internal electric field
Synergistic effect
Photocatalysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic schematic of MBC. SEM images of (b) BOB, (c) MCS, and (d, e) MBC-2. (f–h) TEM images, (i) HRTEM image, and (j) EDX spectrum and mappings of MBC-2.
(a) XRD patterns; XPS profiles of (b) Mn 2p, (c) Cd 3d, (d) Bi 4f and S 2p, (e) Br 3d and (f) C 1s on MCS, BOB and MBC-2.

(a) TC photo-degradation plots and (b) the corresponding plots of ln(C 0/C) versus time of various catalysts; (c) Comparison of the catalytic performance of MBC-2 and other developed catalysts; (d) TC destruction efficacies over MBC-2 at diverse pH values; (e) Catalytic efficacies of MBC-2 in the presence of various anions; (f) TC degradation in various water environments; (g) Recycling tests of MBC-2 in the destruction of TC; (h) TOC test; (i) Photocatalytic TC abatement efficacy of MBC-2 with diverse scavengers.
(a) Degradation routes of TC; (b–d) Toxicity appraisement.
(a, b) Wettability tests on BOB and MBC-2; (c) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of MCS, BOB and MBC-2; (d) Photocurrent densities, (e) EIS profiles, and (f) PL curves of samples.
(a) UV–vis DRS of BOB, MCS, MCS/BOB and MBC-2; (b) Optical bandgaps, (c, d) Mott‒Schottky plots, (e) band structure diagram, and (f) UPS analysis of BOB and MCS.

XPS spectra of (a) Bi 4f and S 2p, (b) Cd 3d, and (c) Mn 2p for MBC-2 in darkness and light illumination; EPR spectra of (d) •O2 – and (e) •OH over MBC-2; Calculated work functions of (f) BOB and (g) MCS; (h, i) Calculated 3D and 2D charge density disparity distribution.
Schematic illustration of TC photodegradation over MBC-2.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots and interfacial chemical bond synergistically modulated S-scheme Mn0.5Cd0.5S/BiOBr photocatalyst for efficient water purification

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: eInstitute of Microstructure and Property of Advanced Materials, Beijing University of Technology, Beijing 100124, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Devising robust S-scheme photocatalysts is of central importance for achieving high-efficient micropollutant decontamination. However, the conscious optimization of S-scheme system with high performance remains a prime challenge. Herein, carbon quantum dots (CDs) and Mn0.5Cd0.5S (MCS) are mounted on BiOBr (BOB) microspheres, establishing an advanced S-scheme heterojunction with interfacial Bi-S bond. The interfacial Bi-S bonds function as superb channels at atomic-scale to abate the energy barrier for S-scheme charge transportation. Meanwhile, CDs serve as electron collectors to preserve highly reductive electrons from MCS, further augmenting the spatial separation of photo-carriers. Therefore, the optimized CDs/MCS/BOB (MBC) heterojunction manifests significantly strengthened tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) destruction activity and its reaction rate constant is approximately 3.1, 2.2, 2.1, and 1.5 folds that than that of MCS, BOB, BOB/CDs and MCS/BOB. In addition, MBC exhibits high stability and significant resistance to environmental interferences. The toxicology evaluation confirms the effective abatement of toxicity of TC after treatment. This achievement demonstrates the benefits of CDs-optimized S-scheme photosystems with chemical bonds for photocatalytic water decontamination.

ce:section.ce:para: With the fast development of industrialization, an ever-increasing quantity of emerging organic pollutants (e.g., dyes, heavy metals, drugs, and pharmaceuticals) have been produced and dumped into aquatic ecosystems, largely deteriorating the natural water environment. Particularly, antibiotics are deemed as harmful pollutants of an emerging concern. Their global overuse in aquaculture and human healthcare has given rise to antibiotic resistance etc., potentially endangering the ecosystem and humans [1-4]. In order to solve this issue, implementation of sustainable wastewater decontamination techniques is of utmost significance [5-9].

Solar-powered photocatalytic technique provides a pragmatic strategy for decomposition of toxic contaminants into harmless substances, destruction of bacteria and production of green energy due to its sustainability, eco-friendliness, cost-effectiveness, and high efficacy [10-24]. To achieve high treatment effectiveness, it is imperative to legitimately devise exceptional photocatalysts [14,25-37]. Bismuth oxybromide (BOB), a prevalent bismuth-based semiconductor with heterolayered architecture, has been identified as a promising photocatalyst with visible-light response and intense oxidation power for pollutant destruction [38]. The distinctive occupation of Bi 6 s and O 2p orbitals on the valence band of BOB is favorable for bandgap optimization and photo-carrier mobility [39-43]. Nevertheless, the photocatalytic ability of unitary BOB is hampered by the severe reunion of photo-carriers.

To promote the activity of photocatalysts, various strategies have been established, including cocatalyst decoration, heteroatom doping, defect or microstructure modulation, and heterojunction design [44-65]. Impressively, with fostered carrier segregation and reinforced redox power of the whole system, S-scheme heterojunctions are ideal candidates for improving the catalytic effectiveness [54,56,58,66-73]. In our recent research, the developed MIL-101(Fe)/BiOCl S-scheme heterojunction was still subjected to unsatisfactory photo-carrier segregation, resulting from its physically attached heterointerface [56]. To further optimize the property of S-scheme systems, efficient diminishment of photo-carrier reunion remains a significant challenge. Establishing S-scheme heterojunction with interfacial chemical bonds is deemed as a practical route to boost spatial separation of carriers [71,72]. In addition, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have been extensively used in combination with photocatalysts to strengthen photoactivity by virtue of their excellent conductivity and broad spectral response [74-79]. Therefore, combining the S-scheme junction construction and CDs modification is a promising strategy to significantly reinforce the catalytic performance.

Mn0.5Cd0.5S (MCS), especially its nanoparticles, has been a promising photocatalyst due to its remarkable surface area, excellent reactivity and intense reduction capacity. However, its limited photocatalytic oxidation ability and susceptibility to photocorrosion pose significant challenges to photocatalytic applications [80-84]. Fortunately, MCS has a more negative energy band level and superior reduction capacity with respect to BOB, making it an ideal candidate to integrate with BOB to establish the S-scheme heterostructure.

Enlightened by the above aspects, CDs/MCS nanodots were embedded in BOB microflowers to create a CDs-regulated S-scheme photocatalyst of MBC for photocatalytic antibiotic eradication. The CDs optimized S-scheme photosystem enables the fostered segregation of electrons and holes while maximizing its redox ability. Moreover, the intense interfacial connection significantly boosts the S-scheme photo-carrier redistribution. The synergy between CDs and chemical bonded S-scheme contributes to the augmented performance of antibiotic destruction over MBC. This exploration gives an enlightening insight into the synergy of chemically bonded S-scheme junction and CDs modulation for upgrading the catalytic properties of materials.
The synthetic procedure of the MBC heterojunction is illustrated in Fig. 1(a) .

Preparation of MCS: Briefly, 1 mmol of L-cystine was introduced into 50 mL of deionized water while stirring. Subsequently, NaOH (6 mol/L) was dropped into the suspension to control its pH at 10.6, making the suspension turn into a clear solution. Next, 25 mL of solution comprised of Cd(AC)2·2H2O (0.5 mmol) and Mn(AC)2·2H2O (0.5 mmol) was added, followed by stirring for 1 h. Afterwards, the mixture was placed in a Teflon reactor and heated at 130 °C for 10 h. After being rinsed with deionized water sufficiently and drying in an electric oven at 60 °C overnight, MCS powder was obtained.

Preparation of MBC: Typically, 1.5 mmol of KBr was dissolved in 12 mL of ethanol and 12 mL of ethylene glycol. Thereafter, under vigorous stirring, 12 mL of ethylene glycol solution containing 1.5 mmol of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was dropwise injected into the KBr solution. After thorough blending, 0.0457 g of MCS and a certain amount of CDs were added to the mixed solution and thoroughly mixed by sonication and agitation. Afterwards, the mixture was placed in a Teflon reactor and heated at 150 °C for 10 h. Three different products with CDs loading amounts of 0.001, 0.003 and 0.005 g were labeled as MBC-1, MBC-2, and MBC-3, respectively.
CDs were synthesized based on a reported route with modification [79]. Pristine BOB was also synthesized under the same conditions without the addition of MCS and CDs.

20 mg of catalyst was dispersed in a 90 mL solution of tetracycline hydrochloride (TC) at a concentration of 20 mg/L. The suspension was agitated in the dark for half an hour to enable the complete adsorption between TC and catalyst. Next, a Xenon lamp (300 W, λ > 420 nm, 3.52 mW/cm2) was turned on to trigger the antibiotic decomposition reaction. The temperature of the reaction solution was maintained at 19±2 °C with the assistance of circulating water during the reaction. The concentration of TC was determined by recording the absorbance at the wavelength of 357 nm on a UV–visible spectrophotometer.

A scanning electron microscope (SEM) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM) were adopted to observe the microstructures of MBC, CDs, BOB and MCS. Fig. 1(b) shows that the architecture of BOB is microflower (diameter: 3–5 μm) assembled by 2D nanosheets. MCS appears as a nanoparticle-like structure (Fig. 1(c)). After solvothermal treatment, MCS (size: 10–30 nm) and CDs (size: 3–8 nm) are tightly anchored on the surface of BOB microflowers (Fig. 1(d–h)). Evidently, the decoration of CDs/MCS hardly affects the architecture of BOB. The unique 0D/0D/3D hierarchical architecture promises an intimate contact over a large area between CDs/MCS dots and BOB nanosheets, which is conducive to the effective spatial separation and conservation of photo-carriers (Fig. 1(d, e, g, h)). TEM image manifests that the average size of as-prepared CDs is about 3.8 nm on the basis of the size statistics (Figs. 1(f) and S1 in Supplementary Information). Further analysis by HRTEM displays apparent lattice distances of 0.31 and 0.35 nm in MBC-2, matching with the (101) plane of MCS [80] and (101) facet of BOB (Fig. 1(i)). Besides, energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) image (Fig. 1(j)) ascertains the surface element composition (Mn, Bi, Cd, C, S, Br and O) of MBC-2. The element mappings (Fig. 1(j)) further reveal the uniform dispersion of these atoms throughout the whole microsphere, indicating that MCS and CDs are tightly and uniformly wrapped around the BOB microsphere. The above characterizations evidence the triumphant preparation of MBC.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was conducted to detect the phase composition of materials. As presented in Fig. 2(a) . the diffraction peaks of BOB at 2θ = 25.1°, 31.7°, 32.2°, 46.2°, and 57.1° correspond to the (101), (102), (110), (200), and (212) crystal planes of tetragonal BOB (JCPDS No. 09-0393) [40,55]. The distinct peaks of MCS can be assigned to hexagonal CdS (JCPDS No. 41-1049) and hexagonal MnS (JCPDS No. 40-1289), confirming the successful preparation of MCS solid solution [81,84]. Meanwhile, the MCS/BOB and MBC heterojunctions exhibit almost the identical XRD pattern with BOB, while characteristic peaks related to MCS and CDs are hardly detected, probably arising from their small content in these heterojunctions.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was utilized to further survey the chemical structure of nanomaterials. As displayed in Fig. S2(a), BOB exhibits clear absorption peaks at 509, 764, and 1280 cm–1, which are ascribed to the Bi–O and Bi–Br bonds. For CDs, the absorption peaks at 1195 and 1573 cm–1 are related to C–O and C=C bonds, and the peak at 1396 cm–1 is derived from C–H bond in the incompletely carbonized citric acid. Furthermore, the absorption peak near 1669 cm–1 belongs to the C=O bond of -COOH. In the case of MBC-2, the characteristic peaks of BOB, MCS, and CDs with a certain shift were detected. Besides, the presence of additional peaks at 617 and 1105 cm–1 accredited to the Bi–S bond indicates the formation of Bi–S bond in MBC-2, which is helpful to the interfacial photo-carrier diffusion.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured to illustrate the surface chemical bonding states of BOB, MCS, and MBC-2. The survey spectra in Fig. S2(b) reveal that MBC-2 possesses the characteristic peaks of Bi 4f, Br 3d, O 1s, S 2p, Cd 3d and Mn 2p, in accordance with the EDX outcome (Fig. 1(j)). Fig. 2(b) presents the Mn 2p peaks at 640.4 eV (Mn 2p3/2) and 651.8 eV (Mn 2p1/2) and the satellite peak at 645.5 eV [80,83] in MBC-2, evidencing the presence of Mn2+. The Cd 3d peaks at 404.8 and 411.6 eV in MBC-2 are from Cd 3d5/2 and Cd 3d3/2 [54,85], respectively (Fig. 2(c)). In Fig. 2(d), it is evident that the two peaks of S 2p at 161.2 and 162.9 eV are associated with S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 [86,87], meanwhile the Bi 4f peaks at 158.9 and 164.3 eV are derived from Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 [56,88], confirming the presence of S2‒ and Bi3+ in MBC-2. The Br 3d spectrum in Fig. 2(e) contains two signals of Br 3d5/2 (68.3 eV) and Br 3d3/2 (69.4 eV) [43,89]. The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. S2(c)) can be fitted with three peaks at 529.4, 531.0, and 532.0 eV, which are derived from lattice oxygen, adsorbed oxygen, and adsorbed H2O [56]. The C 1s spectrum of MBC-2 in Fig. 2(f) exhibits four peaks at 284.8, 286.4, 287.5, and 288.4 eV, belonging to C=C, C–O, C=O, and O=C–O bonds, respectively. Notably, compared to BOB, the peaks of Bi, O, and Br in MBC-2 are negatively shifted, while compared to MCS, the peaks of Mn, Cd, and S in MBC-2 are positively shifted. The peak movements certify the preferable transportation of electrons from MCS to BOB, establishing an internal electric field (IEF) at the interface.

Photocatalytic TC destruction tests were implemented under visible light. Control experiment verifies the stability of TC under visible light illumination (Fig. 3(a) ). Neat BOB and MCS manifest poor activities with TC decomposition percentages of 52.8 % and 46.1 % within 60 min, respectively. In comparison, MCS/BOB and CDs/BOB unveil strengthened activities, due to the creation of binary heterojunctions [56]. Encouragingly, after embedding MCS and CDs into BOB microspheres simultaneously, the photodegradation efficiencies increase to 73.5 %, 81.1 %, and 71.8 % over MBC-1, MBC-2, and MBC-3, respectively. Among them, MBC-2 demonstrates the best TC destruction activity with the rate constant of 0.0202 min‒1, which is approximately 2.2, 3.1, 2.1, and 1.5 folds of BOB (0.0091 min‒1), MCS (0.0066 min‒1), CDs/BOB (0.0098 min‒1), and MCS/BOB (0.0131 min‒1), respectively (Fig. 3(b)). As illustrated in Fig. 3(c) and Table S1 in Supplementary Information, MBC-2 outperforms most of various BiOBr-based heterostructured photocatalysts in terms of catalytic effectiveness towards the purification of TC. This activity augment can be attributed to the establishment of CDs optimized MCS/BOB S-scheme junction, which effectively upgrades the migration and separation efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers at the interface [79]. The TC destruction efficacy over MBC first rises and then falls as the CDs content increases, reflecting that excess CDs induce a decrement in photoactivity. This phenomenon could be due to that abundant CDs render a shrinkage in reactive sites and a competition for harvesting photons in MBC [79]. The above outcomes underscore the remarkable effectiveness of rationally devising MBC ternary S-scheme junction in activity augment.

Beyond that, oxytetracycline and naphthalene were also chosen as the target contaminants to assess the universal catalytic competence of MBC-2 (Fig. S3). Impressively, MBC-2 materials still deliver robust catalytic performance toward the destruction of oxytetracycline and naphthalene with destruction efficiencies of 92.4 % and 87.7 % within 75 min of reaction, respectively.

To appraise the adaptability of MBC-2 in real wastewater environments, a series of catalytic experiments were conducted. As depicted in Fig. 3(d), the eradication efficiency of TC over MBC-2 remains above 73 % in both acidic and alkaline environments, reflecting the remarkable pH adaptability of MBC-2. Fig. 3(e) illustrates the influence of anions (PO4 3‒, NO3 ‒, Cl‒, HCO3 ‒, and SO4 2‒) on the catalytic effectiveness of MBC-2. Clearly, PO4 3‒, NO3 ‒, and Cl‒ display a non-significant detrimental effect on the TC photodecomposition, while SO4 2‒ and HCO3 ‒ pose a distinct inhibitory impact on TC removal possibly as a result of their consumption of partial ROS [8]. To further survey the applicability of MBC-2, TC removal experiments were conducted in different practical water matrices. As seen in Fig. 3(f), the TC abatement efficacy is reduced in river (76.0 %) and sea water (72.4 %), mainly attributed to the scavenging of ROS by coexisting interfering substances in the actual water environment [55,79]. Five runs of recovery experiments were conducted in order to appraise the stability of the MBC-2. As shown in Fig. 3(g), after 5 h (5 cycles), nearly 91 % of the original photoactivity was maintained, and the TC destruction efficacy still reached 74.4 % at the fifth run. The FTIR images, XRD patterns and SEM characterization (Fig. S4) reveal that the MBC-2 powders reclaimed after cycling reaction manifest identical chemical structures and microstructures compared with the original one, reflecting the robust stability. As exhibited in Fig. 3(h), the total organic carbon (TOC) of the TC solution was also analyzed, and the final TOC abatement efficiency over MBC-2 reached 56.1 %, manifesting MBC-2 possesses the prominent mineralization capability.

To unclose the role of various reactive species in photoreaction, a series of trapping experiments were implemented over MBC-2 (Fig. 3(i)). The introduction of TEMPO, IPA and EDTA-2Na inhibits the destruction of TC, making the abatement efficiency drop from 81.1 % to 60.8 %, 69.8 % and 12.2 %, respectively. This phenomenon demonstrates the indispensable roles of h +, •O2 – and •OH for TC eradication by MBC-2.

The degradation process of TC in the MBC-2 photocatalysis system was probed by the determination of intermediates using high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) technique (Fig. S5). The molecular data of TC and its degradation fragments is displayed in Table S2. The decomposition of TC proceeds in two routes. In general, the ROS are inclined to attack the functional groups of –NH2, C=C, and –OH due to their intense electron density. In pathway I, TC undergoes demethylation and hydroxylation, resulting in a production of P1 (m/z = 437), which is transformed into P2 (m/z = 362) and P3 (m/z = 305) through deamination, ring-cleavage, and oxidation reactions. Under the raid of h + and •O2 –, P3 (m/z = 305) experiences ring-breakage, bond fracture and dehydroxylation reactions, generating P4 (m/z = 223). In pathway II, deformamidation, dehydroxylation, ring cleavage, and oxidation reactions occur on TC, resulting in the generation of P5 (m/z = 410), which further experiences demethylation, deamination, oxidation reactions to produce P6 (m/z = 384) and P7 (m/z = 340). •OH and h + species further trigger ring-breakage, and a series of elimination processes, making these byproducts decompose into smaller molecular fragments (P8–P11).

The toxicity evaluation software tool (T.E.S.T.) was applied to judge the potential hazards of these decomposition fragments (Fig. 4 (b, c) and Table S3). In view of that substances with a fathead minnow LC50 lower than 1.0 mg/L are deemed as "toxic" compounds, TC is a "toxic" substance due to its lower LC50 value. All fragments, except for P2, have greater LC50 values than TC, reflecting a decrement in the acute toxicity of TC solutions after photocatalytic treatment (Fig. 4(b)). As seen in Fig. 4(c), except for P9, the developmental toxicity of the fragments is smaller than that of TC (0.89 mg/L). Thereinto, P6 is a developmental non-toxicant with its value lower than 0.5 mg/L. For the mutagenicity toxicity (Fig. 4(d)), compared with TC that defined as "mutagenicity positive", P1, P2, P4, and P8-P11 are transformed into "mutagenicity negative" molecules. These outcomes indicate that the toxicity of TC has been significantly decreased after treatment by MBC-2. On account of there is toxic fragments, a longer treatment period is suggested to achieve the efficacious conversion of TC into non-toxic molecules.

The hydrophilic feature of catalysts as one of the significant factors determining the photoreaction performance deserves to be evaluated. Fig. 5 (a, b) presents the wettability photographs of BOB and MBC-2. Clearly, the water contact-angle of MBC-2 (54.4°) is much smaller than that of BOB (119.5°), reflecting that the embedding of MCS/CDs into BOB optimizes the hydrophilic property of BOB, which is favorable to the photocatalytic decontamination of TC solutions. The surface areas of BOB, MCS and MBC-2 were also analyzed by recording their N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms. All catalysts reveal type IV isotherms with H3 hysteresis loops, certifying the appearance of mesopores (Fig. 5(c)). Moreover, the BET specific surface area of MBC-2 is 37.32 m2 g−1, significantly larger than that of BOB (22.03 m2 g−1) and MCS (31.74 m2 g−1). The larger surface area of MBC-2 is conducive to capturing pollutants and improving the reactive regions, thus promoting photocatalytic pollutant decontamination.

The dynamics of photo-carrier separation and migration upon photoexcitation were studied via photoelectrochemical measurements [55,56,59]. The analysis of transient photocurrent response reveals a significant enhancement in the photocurrent intensity of MBC-2 compared to BOB, MCS and the binary MCS/BOB heterojunction (Fig. 5(d)). This indicates that the introduction of an appropriate amount of CDs helps to generate more charge carriers and improves the efficiency of photo-generated electron-hole separation. The charge transport behavior of MBC-2 was revealed through electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) [5]. As shown in Fig. 5(e), MBC-2 has the smallest arc diameter among the tested catalysts, confirming a significantly enhanced interfacial charge transfer capability upon the incorporation of CDs into MCS/BOB. The photo-carrier dissociation dynamics were further traced by photoluminescence spectroscopy (Fig. 5(f)). Evidently, the weakest PL emission intensity of MBC-2 illustrates the cardinal role of CDs-optimized S-scheme junction in impeding the reunion of photo-carriers.

To better comprehend the origin of the superior activity of MBC-2, the sunlight responsive capacities of BOB, MCS, MCS/BOB, and MBC-2 were also probed by UV–Vis DRS. As seen in Fig. 6(a) , BOB and MCS exhibit characteristic absorption edges at 450 and 559 nm, respectively [40,55,84]. The light absorption capacity of the MCS/BOB heterojunction falls between that of BOB and MCS. After CDs modification, the visible-light absorption capacity of MBC-2 is significantly improved. The optimization of light absorption is helpful to the effective production of photo-carriers and reinforcement of catalytic effectiveness.

The band structures of the heterojunction were further elucidated. The band gaps (E g) of BOB and MCS, obtained from the Tauc plots in Fig. 6(b), are approximately 2.71 and 2.10 eV, respectively, coincident with the reported results [55,87]. Mott-Schottky (M-S) spectra in Fig. 6(c, d) determine the flat-band potentials (E fb) of MCS and BOB at −0.70 and −0.44 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively, which can be converted to −0.50 and −0.24 V (vs. NHE) through the Nernst formula: E NHE = E Ag/AgCl + 0.197. In view of the E fb is positioned at 0.1 V below its E CB for n-type semiconductors, thus the conduction band potentials (E CB) of MCS and BOB are −0.60 and −0.34 V vs. NHE, respectively. Accordingly, their valence band potentials (E VB) are determined to be 2.37 and 1.50 V, respectively. DRS and M-S analyses affirm the establishment of the staggered band alignment of MCS and BOB (Fig. 6(e)).

To further elucidate the motion of interfacial charges, the work functions (Φ) of MCS and BOB were detected using ultraviolet photoelectron spectroscopy (UPS). It is worth noting that the Φ value of BOB (5.96 eV) is greater than that of MCS (4.63 eV) (Fig. 6(f)), indicating that when they join together in a heterojunction, electrons in MCS readily flood into BOB across the interface until their E f achieves a balance, establishing an internal electric field (IEF), in line with the outcome of XPS (Fig. 2).

To get further insight into the S-scheme carrier redistribution mechanism within MBC-2, in situ irradiated XPS measurements, EPR analyses and DFT simulations were executed. Fig. 7 (a–c) displays the in situ XPS spectra of MBC-2. Clearly, under light illumination, the peaks of Bi 4f experience discernable positive movements, while S 2p, Mn 2p and Cd 3d signals experience blueshifts compared with those in darkness. The above outcome discloses the transportation of photo-excited electrons from BOB to MCS, thus verifying the S-scheme photocatalysis mechanism.

Moreover, EPR measurements were carried out to delve deeper into the charge migration mechanism. As seen in Fig. 7(d, e), both MCS and BOB deliver emblematic •O2 – signal by virtue of their E CB being more negative than that of O2/•O2 – (−0.33 V). BOB manifests a distinct •OH signal because of its more positive E VB compared with that of OH–/•OH (1.99 V), while no •OH signal is observed over MCS, revealing its inability to generate •OH. Noteworthily, the MBC-2 displays an appreciably intensified signal of •O2 – and •OH compared with BOB and MCS, signifying that the photo-carrier transport style complies with the S-scheme route instead of type II mode [55]. Evidently, the unique S-scheme charge separation behavior in MBC-2 can accomplish the effective photo-generation of •O2 – and ·OH radicals thermodynamically from the thermodynamics principle.

Additionally, DFT simulations (Fig. 7(f, g)) reveal the Φ values of BOB and MCS are 5.01 and 4.48 eV, which coincides with the experimental outcomes of UPS (Fig. 6(f)). Such divergence in Φ values enables charges to drift from MCS to BOB until their E f balance, thus making their band edge curved and producing an IEF.

The 3D charge density discrepancy was attained and presented in Fig. 7(h). The yellow and cyan regions reflect the charge accumulation and depletion, respectively. It is evident that the BOB surface was primarily comprised of yellow, with some cyan regions. On the contrary, the MCS surface was dominantly occupied by cyan regions, with partial yellow areas. The above findings certify that MCS and BOB function as charge donators and acceptors, respectively. This scenario is further corroborated by the 2D mapping of charge density variation (Fig. 7(i)), providing compelling evidence for the S-scheme charge movement mode in MBC-2, which is consistent with above XPS, in situ XPS, and EPR results.

Based on the above discoveries, a reasonable mechanism for the photodegradation of antibiotics over S-scheme MBC heterojunction is proposed (Fig. 8 ). The work function of the semiconductor plays a pivotal role in the interfacial charge transfer. The divergence in work function results in a directed IEF from MCS to BOB, where the IEF between MCS and BOB gives rise to the bending of the energy bands, with those of MCS bending upwards and those of BOB bending downwards. Under light illumination, the crooked bands and strong IEF offer a powerful force to propel the photo-excited electrons on the CB of BOB to jump into the VB of MCS, resulting in the reunion of useless electrons and holes. Such photo-carrier transport process obeys S-scheme mechanism, while the 0D/0D/3D heterostructure with intense interfacial interaction further promotes the S-scheme photo-carrier redistribution [79,90,91]. Moreover, the CDs also make a significant contribution to the optimized light responsiveness and boosted separation of charge carriers through fostering the enrichment of highly reductive electrons and oxidative holes in CDs and VB of BOB, respectively [79,92,93]. To sum up, the combined effects of interfacial chemical bonds, S-scheme photo-carrier separation mode, and CDs modification collaboratively improve the production and utilization of photo-carriers, leading to the efficient photocatalytic antibiotic decontamination performance of MBC. The primary photoreactions can be summarized as follows:
MBC + hv → MBC (e –, h +) (1)
h + (MCS) + e – (BOB) → Recombination (2)
h + (BOB) + H2O or OH– → •OH (3)
e – (CDs) + O2 → •O2 – (4)
•O2 – (CDs)/h + and •OH (BOB) + TC → CO2 + H2O + Small molecules (5)

In summary, S-scheme heterojunction MBC with chemical bonding interface is devised for efficient photocatalytic antibiotic destruction in water. The chemically bonding S-scheme hetero-interface decorated with CDs enables efficient segregation and amassment of energetic photo-carriers for photocatalysis, leading to about a 3.1-, 2.2-, 2.1-, and 1.5-fold improvement in photocatalytic TC elimination rate as compared to the MCS, BOB, BOB/CDs and MCS/BOB. Besides, the MBC demonstrates excellent recycling stability and anti-interference properties. The toxicology evaluation reveals the high efficacy of the MBC/visible light system in diminishing the toxicity of tetracycline hydrochloride. This study emphasizes the high effectiveness of CDs modification and chemical-bonding interface in developing high-performance photocatalysts.

Fang Yang: Software, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition. Yanping Liu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Lina Bai: Methodology, Resources, Software, Visualization. Mingyi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Chunqiang Zhuang: Writing – review & editing, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
